title_en: Global Expansion and Thoughts on Advertising
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Global Expansion and Thoughts on Advertising." To Go Far Today there's no particular conclusion — I just want to share what I've been thinking about lately. Masu
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Global Expansion and Thoughts on Advertising"

as today's theme.

▼To Go Far

Today there's no particular conclusion — I just want to share what I've been thinking about lately.

It's been three years since I launched the masu brand "FOMUS,"

and at this point, I'm taking on the challenge of going international (Europe).

To do that, I myself, as the representative of FOMUS,

need to become someone people find interesting,

even in completely unfamiliar places and environments.

This is something I realized while running the brand —

when it comes to expanding FOMUS,

"MaSU" always comes as part of the package.

If people find MaSU interesting,

they'll find FOMUS interesting too.

Wherever I am,

"MaSU" matters more than FOMUS itself.

That means I need to become a more appealing and interesting person to those around me.

And for that,

communication is essential.

That's exactly why

I'm currently staying in Cebu for three months,

doing intensive English training at a rapid pace.

Because

if I can communicate in English,

I can reach more people and

get them to know the FOMUS brand.

With only Japanese, we can only expand within Japan.

But if I can speak English,

I can reach far more people.

However, even if I become proficient in English,

that doesn't mean things will spread easily.

There are specific roles to be played.

Launching projects,

planning things,

leading the charge,

spreading the word —

there are different roles for each of these.

I've delegated a lot of things

that I can't do myself.

And I've invested my money and time

into things only I can do.

That's why FOMUS has been able to survive.

And now, the international stage.

Actually, in Malaysia, I went in completely solo,

took Masu Photo shoots, and even turned them into a photo book.

*Only for 10 days

I understand that you can't really call that "going international,"

but honestly, it was a trip that confirmed FOMUS has potential.

I used Instagram ads for promotion, gathered people, and did the shoots.

I was able to do the shoots with almost no cost.

That approach is still working for me now,

and I'm using the same strategy for Masu Photo shoots in Cebu.

However, as I wrote before,

the issue is influence.

After all,

to expand,

you need "people whose role is to spread the word."

That's why, this time,

I made an offer to a hugely popular pair of twins in the Philippines.

Not for a Masu Photo shoot,

but as ambassadors for the accessory line "Parure."

As a result, I was able to feature

their photos on the official website,

and for people in the Philippines, seeing "that famous pair representing a brand" raises FOMUS up a notch in prestige.

This isn't aimed at Japanese people at all —

it's targeted at Filipino users.

*It's like how only Japanese people would be impressed by HikaKin — people in other countries don't know him, so the impact doesn't translate

But when it comes to expanding the FOMUS brand globally,

rather than spending 300,000 yen on masu advertising,

I thought it would be better to spend that 300,000 yen on hiring models to help spread the word,

because that leaves something lasting.

Things like

being featured on the website, appearing on various social media, and so on.

Temporary ads are effective for getting people to know the brand, but they don't leave anything behind.

To go far,

I felt the need to rethink how we spend our money.

This time it's about advertising.

FOMUS does have an advertising budget set aside,

but after coming to Cebu and going through trial and error,

we ended up going with the famous twins from the Philippines.

I'm glad we were able to work with the models I wanted most.

Other well-known accessory brands also

use celebrities,

so I want to make sure we nail that

"aspirational" factor properly.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Participating in a Fashion Show." Connections So today, I participated in a fashion show in Cebu as the masu accessory brand "FOMUS Parure.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Participating in a Fashion Show"

as today's theme.

▼Connections

So today, I participated in a fashion show in Cebu

as the masu accessory brand "FOMUS Parure."

Actually,

there's a Cebu version of WSB (a dance competition held around the world), and the model I shot with last week is participating as an organizer for the fashion show segment of the event.

So I had an online meeting with the event representative, and they offered to have the masu accessories featured.

*In English, of course

For FOMUS, being able to participate in an overseas event is incredibly valuable, so I want to cherish opportunities like this.

Being able to speak English

allowed me to seize this kind of chance.

*Last year I wouldn't have been able to

You never know where opportunities will come from,

but as long as you keep moving forward,

chances will come your way.

Coming to Cebu

and staying active is what led to this opportunity.

This time, the two models I shot with last week

plus two additional models wore the accessories.

To be honest, if you ask whether FOMUS Parure

gained recognition from the fashion show,

the effect was probably minimal.

*Since it was just one segment of the event

However, for FOMUS Parure,

the fact that we participated in an event like this,

and more importantly, that I was able to connect with the representative who organizes events worldwide — that was huge.

He asked for FOMUS Parure samples,

so I gave him an entire box.

I have a small bit of hope that this will lead to further expansion.

That said, I won't rest on my laurels —

I'm going all in on expanding FOMUS and Parure worldwide.

▼Eating Local Filipino Food

After the event,

I went out for Filipino food with some of the models.

We had that thing where you eat seafood with your hands.

*I forgot the name

It was so fun and delicious!

After that, we changed locations, had drinks at a bar, and called it a night.

Did everything go according to plan?

Not exactly,

but the biggest takeaway from today

was connecting with the event representative.

It reminded me how important it is to throw yourself into unfamiliar worlds.

Things that didn't go as planned today:

- The fashion show was significantly delayed, and I waited over 4 hours in the heat *the main event was dance

- There was almost no time to prepare putting on the masu accessories

- After the event, instead of walking the 10-minute distance to the restaurant, we waited over 20 minutes for a Grab

Looking at it this way,

everything was related to "time."

I guess I really do hate waiting. Ha!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "I Turned 29." Celebrating a Birthday Overseas So, I think the title says it all, but I turned 29. It's my first time spending a birthday overseas.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "I Turned 29"

as today's theme.

▼Celebrating a Birthday Overseas

So, I think the title says it all,

but I turned 29.

This is my first time spending a birthday overseas.

That said, I'm not really the type who enjoys being celebrated in a big way, so I didn't do anything particularly birthday-like.

I went to karaoke with friends,

had dinner, and finished the night drinking at a bar.

For the first time in a while,

I took a break from work and enjoyed a day off.

It had been about five years since I last went to karaoke.

It made me wonder if this is the kind of day off

normal people enjoy all the time.

Then again, if it weren't my birthday,

I probably wouldn't have gone to karaoke either. Ha!

▼Countdown to 30

Now that I'm 29,

there's just one year left until 30.

The special time called "my twenties"

is almost over.

I've reached an age where

I really need to produce results.

To expand the masu brand "FOMUS" to the world,

I felt I needed "English" skills,

which is why I'm here in Cebu right now.

While improving my English skills,

on my days off, I interact with local models through photo shoots.

This is something I could only do now, having launched the FOMUS brand and having photography skills. Starting at 29 might seem late, but personally, I feel it worked out well.

Rather than studying English vaguely,

having a clear reason to learn

makes a huge difference in commitment.

As a result, what I learn on weekdays

gets put to use during weekend model shoots.

Furthermore, being able to casually communicate in English has led to something different from before.

That is,

the birthday messages I received were in English.

Friends overseas and models

sent me tons of messages on social media.

Especially from the Filipino models —

I received so many messages.

I got plenty of birthday messages from my Japanese friends too, but being celebrated by overseas friends as well was really heartwarming.

At 29, I'll make sure to produce solid results overseas,

and in my 30s, while traveling between Japan and abroad,

I'll continue as a photographer and with FOMUS,

creating an environment where people involved with us

can feel even more excited about life!!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Lessons with the Head Coach." At This Timing! My time in Cebu is almost over. Only two more weeks of classes. And at this timing,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Lessons with the Head Coach"

as today's theme.

▼At This Timing!

My time in Cebu is almost over.

Only two more weeks of classes.

And at this timing,

I got a message from

the language school manager.

It said that a class slot from 5:15 PM to 6:00 PM had become available.

Until now, classes were basically from 8 AM to 5 PM,

but one additional period was added.

That said, since the number of daily periods is fixed,

if I wanted to switch, I'd have to swap out one of my current classes.

The message included a list of available teachers,

and right at the top was the name of the school's head coach!

The head coach is usually too busy to teach, so he's in a non-teaching position, but for some reason, just one slot opened up!

I jumped at the chance

and submitted my change request as fast as possible.

Because it was first come, first served.

As a result, starting this week, I have one-on-one lessons with the head coach!! Ha!

For someone like me who wants to improve

both English skills and business acumen,

there's no better person at this school.

I actually took the class, and

it was incredibly easy to understand, as expected!

He doesn't just accurately point out my mistakes and correct them —

he immediately asks me to form the correct sentence,

testing on the spot whether I truly understood.

He already knew about me,

but by formally introducing myself again,

he learned more about my activities,

and we could tailor the class content to match.

There's a good level of tension,

and it's fun in a different way from the other teachers.

The 45-minute period

flew by incredibly fast.

I felt like I was being thoroughly trained.

At this school, I kept swapping teachers

to find the ones that suited me best.

Around this time, a huge wave of new students came in,

so there were already few teachers available to switch to.

In my case, my starting lineup is complete,

so I won't be making any more changes,

but I learned a lot from swapping so many times.

In business too, reading people is important.

There are many situations where you need to assess collaboration partners or business associates in a single meeting.

I feel like I've been honing those skills

here in Cebu as well, without even trying.

▼Get What You Can Get

The head coach

teaches the other teachers how to teach,

so his teaching ability is top-notch!

Honestly, I wish I could have had him earlier, but there's nothing I can do about that.

Instead, I'm grateful to have him for one period over these last two weeks.

Right now, I'm absorbing everything

I can in Cebu,

with the stance of making it all

part of my foundation,

and that's actually happening.

At this pace,

I'll firmly acquire the skills I need right now

and improve my English to a level I can use in real business settings.

title_en: Things to Watch Out for When Shipping Packages from Japan to Cebu
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Things to Watch Out for When Shipping Packages from Japan to Cebu." Japan and Overseas Are Different This time, based on my own experience, I'd like to share some tips about shipping packages from Japan to Cebu.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Things to Watch Out for When Shipping Packages from Japan to Cebu"

as today's theme.

▼Japan and Overseas Are Different

This time, based on my own experience,

I'd like to share some tips about shipping packages from Japan to Cebu.

The reason I can write this article

is because I actually shipped a package from Japan to Cebu.

The story goes back about a month.

I was running low on

contact lenses,

and I had used up all my

Pabron medicine by February,

so I had contacts and Pabron

shipped from Japan to Cebu.

I used "EMS," which is supposed to be the fastest delivery option.

I've sent masu and photo books to Taiwan a few times before, and packages arrive very quickly.

Following the same process,

I addressed the package to myself at the language school in Cebu and shipped it.

*You enter the address and everything online

*No handwriting involved

A few days after I sent it,

it arrived at the Tokyo International Post Office

and was sent off to the Philippines.

When shipping from Japan to the Philippines,

it first goes to Manila.

After that, it gets sent from Manila to other islands [in this case, Cebu].

Then,

it's sent to a large post office in Cebu,

forwarded to a smaller local post office,

and from there, delivered to the address on the package.

That's the general flow.

However,

in my case, it took over a month to receive the package.

Here's the tracking history.

The limit of what you can track with the Japanese tracking number

After arriving in the Philippines, you check on the Philippine site

Things moved smoothly on the Japan side.

But once it reached the Philippines, everything was incredibly slow.

On top of that, I found out later

that the tracking number only shows information

up to the point when the package arrives in the Philippines

and is shipped onward to Cebu.

Beyond that, no information comes up.

In my case, I could see that the package was moved on April 5th, but after that — nothing.

Furthermore, in Cebu,

you apparently have to go pick up

packages yourself.

If the package isn't too big,

they'll supposedly deliver it,

but for some reason, it never arrived even though I waited...

So, losing patience,

I called the post office during class with my teacher's help

and got them to look into it.

Ideally I'd handle it on my own,

but the post office staff speak the local language,

which was way too difficult for me.

So I had them check where my package was at the post office, and I went to pick it up that same day.

*The post office was in a small space on the 2nd floor!

It took 1 month and 10 days to get here!

About 5 weeks in total!

During this period,

I wore glasses instead of contacts,

and since I had no medicine,

I was extra careful not to catch a cold.

So there you have it —

you need to be careful when shipping

packages from Japan to Cebu.

Well, to begin with,

if I had brought 3 months' worth of contact lenses

and medicine with me in the first place,

I wouldn't have needed to ship anything.

So I learned that advance preparation is really important.

If you're heading overseas after reading this article,

I strongly recommend preparing early

so you don't run out of anything!

See you next time!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "My First Aromatherapy." After Class So, now that I finally got my package, I'm back to wearing contact lenses.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "My First Aromatherapy"

as today's theme.

▼After Class

So, now that I finally got my package,

I'm back to wearing contact lenses.

Since I have bad eyesight, wearing glasses makes my eyes look smaller, so visually speaking, I "personally" prefer contacts.

Anyway, putting that small talk aside,

after class, I headed to my go-to massage place.

I'm currently taking an elective vocabulary class,

and all my classes end at 7:45 PM.

I arrived at the massage place around 8 PM

and chose from the menu.

*1 peso = 2.6 yen

During my time in Cebu,

I've been to 3 massage places so far,

but this one is the closest to the school.

So I've been coming here a lot.

This time, I wanted to try something I'd never had before,

so I chose "Aromatherapy."

What makes it different from a regular oil massage is

that it has a fragrance,

and instead of oil, they use a liquid.

This time, I had a thorough 1.5-hour session to work out all the knots.

The weekday curfew at the school is 10 PM,

and I got back at 9:50 PM.

If I break curfew any more, there's a chance I could get kicked out before graduation, so it was a bit of a close call! Ha!

One random aside:

I got into massage when I was 20.

When I was in college,

I went to Sri Lanka for a seminar research trip,

and there I experienced Ayurveda (Shirodhara),

which is a treatment where warm oil is poured onto the marma point between your eyebrows for a set period of time,

guiding the brain into a meditative state

and producing a relaxing effect.

Sri Lanka was only my second overseas experience,

but that's when I got hooked on massage.

Since then, whenever I visit Southeast Asia,

I go pretty often.

*Massages in Southeast Asia are cheap

Especially since I started my own business,

my body gets all stiff and tense pretty often,

and massage is really effective.

It takes 1 to 2 hours for the treatment,

but work goes much smoother afterward,

so it's a great way to refresh.

Side note:

After nearly 3 months in Cebu,

weekdays are getting harder

to find new things to take in.

That said, there's only 1 week left,

so I want to give it my all with what I can do right now.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Sharing FOMUS's Vision in English." Changing the Topic for Each Teacher Now, I'd like to talk about my classes again after a while and share some insights I had.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Sharing FOMUS's Vision in English"

as today's theme.

▼Changing the Topic for Each Teacher

Now, I'd like to talk about my classes again after a while

and share some insights I had.

I'm in my 11th week of Cebu life,

and all teacher changes have been finalized.

Aside from the head coach,

I've been with my teachers for a while now,

and I understand

"what kind of person each teacher is."

So,

I need to keep things going in a way

that keeps the teachers motivated too.

The teachers are already working hard

to teach us students,

but to become a bit more of a special student on top of that,

I need to create a state where they think "talking with me is fun."

So basically, I don't use textbooks —

I do my classes entirely through conversation.

At the very beginning, I was using about 7 books,

but before I knew it, I wasn't using any

except for the one textbook in group class.

For each teacher,

I make requests like

"I want to have this kind of conversation" or

"I want you to drill me thoroughly on this topic."

Since I came all the way to Cebu,

and my goal isn't to improve test scores

but to actually be able to speak and listen,

this is the format I've adopted.

*If you're planning to study languages in Cebu in the future, you might want to customize things your own way too.

So between all of that,

I'm intensely practicing what I need right now,

and sometimes teachers spend their own time

preparing business-related discussion topics for me,

making for classes that really work my brain.

In that context, I've placed a business-talk-only order with one teacher,

and I've talked a lot about FOMUS's content and services before,

but at this point, I noticed something.

That is, getting people to imagine the possibilities of FOMUS.

In Japan, we're gradually starting to show that through our actions,

but I'd been searching for a way to get that level of understanding overseas.

But I found something that clicked perfectly, so I want to share it.

▼ Conveying What FOMUS Is Aiming For

FOMUS uses Japan's traditional craft, the "masu," to connect people

and create new value — that's what we do.

I needed to communicate this in English,

and when I tried explaining the following,

it seemed to really click.

That is,

by traveling around myself, I connect people all over the world.

Then, I leverage the strengths of each person I meet,

combine them with FOMUS's own strengths,

create new value,

and give back to the members — that's the story I told.

That's still a bit abstract, so...

For example:

MaSU goes to Cebu for language study (traveling around)

Finds a teacher at the school

Talks about FOMUS

FOMUS members want to learn English (hypothetically)

Asks the teacher to teach English to the members

Gets a yes

FOMUS ENGLISH project is born

FOMUS masu owners can learn English at a discount (and with MaSU's personal stamp of approval)

*What makes this different from Rarejob or Native Camp is that everyone owns a FOMUS masu and shares a connection through FOMUS. The price might be a bit higher, though.

In this way, when you own a FOMUS masu,

life becomes more fun,

you gain access to learning environments,

and you can participate in events.

FOMUS is a community,

and the FOMUS masu is a "communication tool" that connects the community.

This ended up reading like a memo,

but in practice,

this flow (especially the analogy) really clicked,

and the teacher became interested in what FOMUS

is aiming for (its vision) as well.

At FOMUS, each person can leverage their strengths and take on their own role.

I'm good at taking photos and planning things,

but I don't really cook properly, and I can't make short videos either. Accessories are another one of those things.

By working on those areas together with my teammates,

we can each pursue what we want to do

and create things that generate even more value.

Unlike traditional corporate structures,

FOMUS isn't pyramid-shaped —

everyone is on a flat, equal footing,

and because we work with people who act on their own initiative,

we can take action overwhelmingly fast.

If I can communicate all of this properly in English,

I feel like we'll be able to welcome

even more people into FOMUS.

So that's what today was about —

I was able to convey what FOMUS is aiming for

in English.

I've had these kinds of conversations with models before,

but doing it with a teacher was a first,

and it was enjoyable in its own way.

I'll keep building momentum from here.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Consistency and Preparation." Health First Now, I'd like to write about the things I've been mindful of during my stay in Cebu. First, moving my body every day.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Consistency and Preparation"

as today's theme.

▼Health First

Now, I'd like to write about the things

I've been mindful of during my stay in Cebu.

First, moving my body every day.

When I was in Japan, I was living as an address hopper,

constantly on the move, walking at least 10,000 steps a day.

But the roads where I'm staying now are bumpy,

the streets are narrow,

and it's not exactly a great place for walks.

So I regularly run on the treadmill

at the school gym.

As I mentioned in a previous article,

the Medoc Marathon is coming up in September this year.

I need to run 42 km,

so training is essential.

I started around mid-February,

and I've been able to keep it up consistently.

At first, even running 3 km was incredibly tough, but now I can run 5 to 9 km.

The reason business owners tend to hit the gym

isn't just for fitness —

it's probably also because you can see results pretty easily.

It's only been 2 months for me,

but by sticking with it, both my running time and distance have improved dramatically.

My goal is 42 km,

so it's still uncharted territory,

but I'm steadily building up my stamina.

The other thing is waking up early.

I wake up between 6:00 and 6:30 AM every morning.

This is because there's a vocabulary test at 7 AM,

so I need to study for it, which is why I'm up so early.

But being forced into a morning routine has actually been a good thing.

The question is whether I can keep this up in Japan...

I'm sure I'll be fine. Ha!

There are other small things

I've been keeping up with too,

but the habits I started in February have firmly taken root.

I'm reminded once again how important building habits can be.

▼Preparing for After My Return

While I'm in Cebu,

I'm also remotely working on May's schedule and event preparations at the same time.

On May 3rd and 4th, in Sakado City, Saitama Prefecture,

I'm planning a FOMUS digital art exhibition.

Since we'll be displaying various artworks,

I'm also moving forward with printing and purchasing frames.

I'm returning to Japan on the night of April 30th,

so I only have May 1st and 2nd

to prepare in Japan.

*An incredibly tight schedule

The reason I set up this schedule is

to prevent myself from getting too comfortable after returning from the Philippines,

and because holding the art exhibition during Golden Week

increases the chances of more people being able to come.

I'm also in the middle of a crowdfunding campaign,

so there's absolutely zero downtime.

After the event, I plan to travel all around Japan.

2024 is packed with events,

and life has become quite aggressive.

So that's the story —

I'm keeping up with my daily habits in Cebu

while also preparing for my return! That's all for today!

See you next time!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "The Stunning Views of Cebu." Sightseeing in My 11th Week So, it's my last Saturday in Cebu, and at this point, I finally went out for some proper sightseeing!
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "The Stunning Views of Cebu"

as today's theme.

▼Sightseeing in My 11th Week

So, it's my last Saturday in Cebu,

and at this point, I finally went out for some proper sightseeing!

The destinations were "Tops" and

a place called "Temple of Leah."

Apparently they're popular spots where you can see stunning views of Cebu City,

drawing lots of tourists,

and I figured I should see them while I still could.

This time, I went with some Japanese people from the same school.

They're in their 1st week of language study, and I'm in my 11th. Ha!

*That's how little sightseeing I've done

That said, I know my way around transportation

like Grab and taxis within Cebu.

However,

since today's destination was up in the mountains,

I found out the day before that Grab wouldn't work,

so I quickly booked a van with a driver!

*I'm good at this kind of booking

For 5 people total, 2,800 pesos for 4 hours — great deal!

Plus, they came to pick us up at the school,

so we saved both time and money.

Let me look back on the day.

9:30 AM.

The van arrived right on time.

We hopped in and headed to Temple of Leah.

Along the way, we drove up mountain roads.

And after about 30 minutes,

we arrived!

*Built by a wealthy man of Cebu as a symbol of his eternal love for his late beloved wife, Leah

It's not that large,

so 60 minutes is more than enough for sightseeing.

After that, we headed to "Tops," where you can see the stunning views of Cebu.

We had lunch here.

*Apparently they all quit their jobs at the end of March to come here

And the view was stunning!

*I only took video, so here's a group photo instead

*Please see the actual scenery for yourself in person

After that, the tour was over,

but instead of heading back to the school,

we went to "Fort San Pedro," another tourist landmark.

*I really haven't done any sightseeing these 3 months. Ha!

It was on the smaller side,

and compared to Japanese castles, it wasn't tall at all.

The rocks looked like you could just climb right over them.

For the details, look it up yourself.

After the fort,

I said goodbye to the Japanese ladies.

Private car charter: 2,800 pesos

Temple of Leah admission: 800

Tops admission: 550

Lunch: 2,139

All included, it was 1,260 pesos per person,

which is about 3,400 yen.

You could probably go even cheaper

if you tried,

but considering time and everything,

hiring a driver by the hour

is more efficient.

Chartering is the way to go!

After that, I went to a spa to get a massage,

then headed back to school.

I had dinner at school,

and then went to a bar run by a Japanese owner.

I went once during the first week of February,

but I was drinking with friends that time,

so I didn't get a chance to talk to other people.

This time I did.

There aren't that many Japanese-run bars,

so I wish I'd gone more often.

I'm thinking of going again on a weekday.

*On weekends it's crowded and sometimes you can't chat with the owner

And I made it back to school at 11:50 PM, just barely before curfew.

It was a packed day from morning to night!

Who would've thought I'd finally visit the famous tourist spots

in my 11th week? Ha!

This too is part of my Cebu activity log.

I'd like to think I'm showing everyone

a Cebu stay that's a little different from the norm.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "FOMUS Event in Cebu." Spending My Last Day Off with the Masu Models Less than a week left of my 3-month stay in Cebu. Meaning, it's my last Sunday.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "FOMUS Event in Cebu"

as today's theme.

▼Spending My Last Day Off with the Masu Models

Less than a week left of my 3-month stay in Cebu.

Meaning, it's my last Sunday.

So,

I held a FOMUS event

that I'd been planning for a while!

The venue was a Filipino restaurant specializing in lechon.

Its name: "House of Lechon."

I exchanged messages with them a few times on Facebook

and decided on this venue.

Originally, about 8 people were expected to attend,

but between cancellations due to illness

and no-shows,

4 models ended up coming.

One of them was a model I was shooting that day.

I did the shoot before the event and she joined right after!

She's a professional model

whom I met at last week's fashion show.

She wore the masu accessory "FOMUS Parure" at that event and really loved it.

So we decided to do a shoot together this time.

We got some great shots!

And then, the event began!

*Everyone was wearing masu accessories

That said, since there were only 4 people,

we kept it casual and just enjoyed free conversation.

I got to enjoy the food and conversation too.

The models were all meeting each other for the first time.

I was the only one who had met everyone before.

What they had in common was:

they all encountered "FOMUS" and "MaSU"

and had a "Masu Photo" taken.

But that common ground

is a tremendous advantage.

We're not starting from nothing,

and since I'm there too,

the conversation flows naturally.

Also, my English has improved

a little over these past few months,

so I can now convey what I want to say.

However, my listening is still a work in progress.

There were still plenty of parts I couldn't catch.

That's a challenge for this year.

Even so, for communicating,

I'm in perfectly fine shape.

4 Cebu-based models

and 1 Japanese person —

of course, all conversation was in "English."

Being able to create this environment over 3 months (really just on weekends) gives me confidence.

Once again, by simply taking action,

I was able to create an opportunity like this, and I'm glad I did.

And it's not enough for just me to connect with local models —

I also fulfilled the role of "connecting the models with each other."

These connections are what matter in the times ahead,

and they're essential for making

the FOMUS brand stronger.

This is what I believe

"AI can never do — creating memories."

Everyone who has a "Masu Photo" taken

receives a "masu" as a souvenir.

That masu has the FOMUS logo on it.

A masu with the FOMUS logo

has no meaning on its own,

but the "Masu Photo," "meeting MaSU,"

and "getting together as masu models" —

those are facts that will never disappear.

These "memories" and "connections"

are born precisely because they happen in real life,

and they require an investment of money and time.

I learned this firsthand in Japan,

and I'm applying the same approach here in Cebu.

For them too,

it has become a special Japanese item.

Of course, through FOMUS, they get to experience the Japanese tradition of "masu,"

and these experiences and encounters

have a value that money can't buy.

I treasure this deeply.

If you ask whether there's

some big immediate benefit,

maybe not right now.

But the fact that we met at this moment has meaning,

and I believe that years or even decades from now, the connections made in Cebu will lead to something.

Let's check back on that answer in 10 years.

Once again, it was an incredibly meaningful event.

▼You Have to Make Your Own Life Choices the Right Ones

After the event, we were supposed to go our separate ways,

but everyone still had time,

so we chatted at a cafe in a nearby mall.

Just being able to speak English (a little) has

made communication overwhelmingly easier,

and I was reminded once again of how important English is.

I'm truly grateful I realized this at this point in time.

Looking back, this is the interpretation I've come to:

Because I never seriously studied English

until I was 28,

I got humbled in Malaysia last year,

which is why I'm now in Cebu studying English.

And by using my weekends here,

I've been able to expand the FOMUS brand.

This isn't like studying abroad in college,

and it's a bit different from typical working-adult study abroad, too.

For me, this was truly the best possible timing for language study — I believe that from the bottom of my heart.

As a result, I got to know about 30 people in Cebu and Zamboanga combined through Masu Photo shoots, spreading awareness of the FOMUS brand and making friends along the way.

If you want to meet fellow Japanese people,

you can just go to a Japanese bar.

That's a great initiative too.

But building connections with local people

when you're not even living there permanently

is quite difficult.

I overcame that hurdle using the cards in my hand —

photography and the masu.

*Improved English skills played a part too

As I've written many times in this activity log,

on the surface, FOMUS is a masu brand,

but it's really a "community" wearing the skin of a masu brand.

It's an item that can be used for "cheers" — something universal around the world.

It works with alcohol, juice, or even water,

so religion doesn't matter either.

Plus, unlike a glass, it won't break

if you drop it, so it's durable too.

And to give it meaning as

a "communication tool,"

we've tied in services like

Masu Photo, events,

and masu accessories.

This is all possible because there are people

in Japan who support and engage with FOMUS,

and the energy and connections born from that

are what I want to properly give back.

So that's the story —

my last Sunday in Cebu

turned out to be an incredibly meaningful time!
